---
title: "Genetics Project Progress"
author: "Group Members: Natalie Karten, Lorna Darling, and Brianna Arledge"
format: html
bibliography: Exported Items.bib
csl: nature.csl
---

## Project Goals
Our project aims to write a program to record the voltage output from a scientific instrument as a function of time, and to plot the output in a graph. We will be making three variations of graphs. 

Brianna Arledge's goal is to write the code for the linear variation of the graph.

## Linear Graph
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd 
df = pd.read_csv('Methyl Stearate.xy', sep = r"\s+", engine = "python", header = None)
df.columns = ["Time", "Voltage"]
plt.plot (df["Time"], df["Voltage"])
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Voltage")
plt.xlim(18, 19)
plt.ylim(46, 56)
plt.title("Methyl Stearate Peak")
plt.show()
```

# The matplotlib and the pandas are libaries and they are being imported on lines 16 and 17. The matplot is used to make the graph while the pandas is used to load and organize the data into the graph. 
# Line 18 creates a DataFrame. It has pd.read_csv to read the data file Methyl Stearate.xy, while sep = r"\s+" indicates that the data values are seperated by one or several spaces. Having the engine equal to python let's the program interpret the inconsistant spacing in the file, while having the header equal none to show the file starts with data and does not contain column titles. 
# Lines 19 through 26 create the plot. The df.columns label the two columns in the file to be set as x and y values. The plt.plot plots the columns on an x-axis and a y-axis. Lines 21 and 22 create the labels for the x and y axis. A specific peak of the plot is zoomed in by using the xlim and ylim to define the start and stop point of the x- and y-axis. The plot is given the title of Methyl Stearate Peak and is told to show the plot.
# The peak on the plot stands for methyl stearate and will be used to determine the area under the peak as well as the retention time. 
[@matplotlib_pyplot_2025]

## Bar Graph, this is the graph I plan on coding for - natie karten
```{r}
##This is installing ggplot2 to create graphics
install.packages("ggplot2")
library(ggplot2)

##This is the data used
data <- read.csv("Methyl_Stearate_Use.csv")

##This was installed to help take averages of the data so that it could be graphed in a bar graph
install.packages("dplyr")
library(dplyr)

data <- data %>%
  mutate(interval = floor(Time))
##This rounded all of the time values down to the nearest second so that there were less catagories needed for the graph
avolt <- data %>%
  group_by(interval) %>%
  summarize(mean_voltage = mean(Voltage))
##This took the average of all the voltage readings in each 1 second interval 

##This created a bar graph for the data using the average voltages and 1 second intervals.
ggplot(avolt, aes(x = factor(interval), y = mean_voltage, fill = factor(interval))) + 
  geom_col()
coord_flip ()
##This was done to put the time on the x axis
title = "Average Voltage in 1 Second Intervals for Methyl Stearate"
labs(x = "Time in Seconds", y = "Average Voltage", fill = "Time in Seconds")
##clarity for the graph

##This created a bar graph for the data using the average voltages and 1 second intervals.
ggplot(avolt, aes(x = factor(interval), y = mean_voltage, fill = factor(interval))) + 
  geom_col()
coord_flip ()
##This was done to put the time on the x axis
title = "Average Voltage in 1 Second Intervals for Methyl Stearate"
labs(x = "Time in Seconds", y = "Average Voltage", fill = "Time in Seconds")
##clarity for the graph

data <- data %>%
  mutate(interval2 = floor(Time * 100) / 100)
##splits the data into .01 second intervals
avolt2 <- data %>%
  group_by(interval2) %>%
  summarize(mean_voltage2 = mean(Voltage, na.rm = TRUE))
##averaging the voltage amounts in each interval

avolt2_subset <- avolt2 %>%
  filter(interval2 >= 18.3 & interval2 <= 18.7)
##Sets the values on the bottom of the graph to 18.2 to 18.8 seconds

ggplot(avolt2_subset, aes(x = factor(interval2), y = mean_voltage2, fill = factor(interval2))) + 
  geom_col()
##Graphing the zoomed in area

title = "Average Voltage in 1 Second Intervals for Methyl Stearate from 18.2 seconds to 18.8"
labs(x = "Time in Seconds", y = "Average Voltage", fill = "Time in Seconds")
##clarity for the graph

area_calculation <- avolt2_subset %>%
  arrange(interval2) %>% #this ensures the output value is positive
  mutate(dx = interval2 - lag(interval2), dy = (mean_voltage2 + lag(mean_voltage2)) / 2, trapezoid_area = dx * dy)
##dx is finding the difference between each .01 second interval between 18.3 and 18.7, because that is the interval of x in this avolt subset. dy is finding the difference between the corrisponding average voltage value for each x value, adding the two together, and dividng the two to find the average. dx and dy are then being multipied together. This is findng the area of each trapeziod.
area <- sum(area_calculation$trapezoid_area, na.rm = TRUE)
#This finds the total area of the trapezoids, which is the area under the curve.

print(area)
```

## Raw Surface Area
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load the data
df = pd.read_csv('Methyl Stearate.xy', sep=r"\s+", engine="python", header=None)

# Label columns
df.columns = ["Time", "Voltage"]

# Filter for peak region (18.3 to 18.5)
df_peak = df[(df["Time"] >= 18.3) & (df["Time"] <= 18.5)]

# Compute area under the curve
area = np.trapezoid(df_peak["Voltage"], df_peak["Time"])
print("Area under the curve:", area)

# Plot the data
plt.plot(df_peak["Time"], df_peak["Voltage"], label="Peak")

# Shade the area
plt.fill_between(df_peak["Time"], df_peak["Voltage"], alpha=0.3)

# Label and Format
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Voltage")
plt.title("Methyl Stearate Peak")

plt.xlim(18, 19)
plt.ylim(46, 56)

plt.legend()
plt.show()
```
# In Gas Chromatography, peak area is proportional to the amount of compound present which is used for quantitative analysis. 
# The area was initially calculated directly from the raw signal, but a more accurate measurement would have been done if a baseline correction was coded for. Since there was no baseline correction, the measured area was higher than expected. 
# There were three imported libraries that was used. matplotlib.pyplot was used to make the graph, pandas was used to read/ organize the data, and numpy was used for calculations. This was to visualize and calculate the area.
# The data was uploaded from the CSV. The columns were then labeled as time was for the retention time and voltage was the detector response. To isolate the methyl stearate peak, the time between 18 and 19 minutes was estimated. 
# The trapezoidal rule was used instead of reimann sums or other ways to calculate the area because it was more accurate. 
# The peak was plotted and the area underneath the peak was shaded for easy visualization. The last lines were dedicated too labeling and formatting the graph. 

## Baseline Correction Surface Area
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('Methyl Stearate.xy', sep=r"\s+", engine="python", header=None)

# Label columns
df.columns = ["Time", "Voltage"]

# Filter for methyl stearate peak (18.3–18.5 min)
df_peak = df[(df["Time"] >= 18.3) & (df["Time"] <= 18.5)]

# Convert to NumPy arrays
x = df_peak["Time"].values
y = df_peak["Voltage"].values

# Create linear baseline
x_start, x_end = x[0], x[-1]
y_start, y_end = y[0], y[-1]

baseline = y_start + (y_end - y_start) * (x - x_start) / (x_end - x_start)

# Subtract baseline
y_corrected = y - baseline

# Compute areas
area_raw = np.trapezoid(y, x)
area_corrected = np.trapezoid(y_corrected, x)

print("Raw area:", area_raw)
print("Baseline-corrected area:", area_corrected)

# Plot
plt.plot(x, y, label="Original Signal")
plt.plot(x, baseline, '--', label="Baseline")

# Shade corrected area
plt.fill_between(x, y_corrected, alpha=0.3, label="Corrected Area")

plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Voltage")
plt.title("Methyl Stearate Peak with Baseline Correction")

plt.xlim(18, 19)
plt.legend()

plt.show()
```
# To do a baseline correction, the start and end points of the peak must be addressed. Creating a linear baseline makes a line that connects the edges of the peak. In Gas Chromatography, most baseline drifts are slow and relatively linear. 
# The baseline needed to be subtracted to only isolate the methyle stearate peak. This removes the baseline value from each data point. Line 177 is important as the area will then only represent the compound signal and not the detector drift. 
# Without the baseline correction, the area is too large which could overestimate the concentration. With the baseline correction, the area is more accurate and he results are reproducible.
